overview.body: This is an ENHANCE unit! You'll copy your Lab 1.1 code and add the power of decisions. Now the player picks a destination, and your program responds differently based on their choice. Did they roll enough miles to make it? That depends on where they're going!

mentorQuote.quote: Before you write any if-elif-else code, think about testing. You've got three valid choices plus invalid input — that's four paths through your code. I always sketch out my test cases first: choice 1, choice 2, choice 3, and something wrong like 99. If you can't test it, you can't trust it!

objectives: Add a destination menu with 3 choices (Corpus, Houston, Austin)
Write an if-elif-else chain to handle each choice (+ invalid)
Set different destination, distance, status, and message variables for each choice
Check whether the player made it using miles_remaining >= 0
Use abs() to show how many miles short (if negative)
Display a Trip Summary showing final results
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 3: Decision Structures and Boolean Logic
programRequirements: Your program MUST start from your Lab 1.1 code
Your program MUST use an if-elif-else chain for 3 choices + else
Your program MUST set destination, distance, status, and message in each branch
Your program MUST use abs() for miles short
Your program MUST include your docstring with name, abc123, section, and description

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_CORPUS
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Corpus Christi (150 miles) — carried over from Lab 1.1

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_HOUSTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Houston (200 miles) — carried over from Lab 1.1

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_AUSTIN
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Austin (80 miles) — carried over from Lab 1.1

rows.task: Copy Lab 1.1, remove Galveston, add menu
Run end-to-end test, verify expected output across all paths, submit to CodeGrade

timeGuide.strategy: Test EVERY branch! Run your program 4 times: once for each menu option (1, 2, 3) and once for an invalid choice (like 5). Every branch of your if-elif-else needs to work correctly.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. You're starting from Lab 1.1 code — the [NEW] tags show what you're adding this unit.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150    (keep from 1.1)
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200   (keep from 1.1)
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80     (keep from 1.1)
# HOU_TO_GALVESTON removed this unit

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
def main():
│
│── Welcome banner, player name, roll (from 1.1)
│── Display roll results
│
│── Display destination menu              [NEW-CP1]
│── choice = int(input('...'))            [NEW-CP1]
│
│── if choice == 1: set Corpus vars      [NEW-CP2]
│── elif choice == 2: set Houston vars   [NEW-CP2]
│── elif choice == 3: set Austin vars    [NEW-CP2]
│── else: set error vars                 [NEW-CP2]
│
│── miles_remaining = miles_rolled - distance  [NEW-CP3]
│── if miles_remaining >= 0:             [NEW-CP3]
│       print arrival message
│   else:
│       miles_short = abs(miles_remaining)
│       print how many miles short
│
│── Display TRIP SUMMARY                 [NEW-CP4]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: A streamlined version of Lab 1.1 with just 3 constants and a destination menu that gets the player's choice.
We're removing the Galveston calculation to keep things simple. The focus of this unit is on decisions, not complex calculations. Galveston returns next unit!

content.quote: Heads up: this is the first time you're working from existing code instead of starting fresh. That's how 90% of professional development actually happens. The trick is to be deliberate about what you trim. Don't delete a constant until you've verified nothing references it. Don't delete the calculation for Galveston until you've removed every line that uses HOU_TO_GALVESTON. Working from a baseline is faster, but it punishes sloppy.

items: Copy your Lab 1.1 code and save it as lab-1-2.py (your main() structure comes with it!)
Update your docstring with the new lab number and description
Remove the HOU_TO_GALVESTON constant and all Galveston calculations
Keep only 3 constants: SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, SAT_TO_AUSTIN
After showing the roll, display a destination menu with 3 options
Get the player's choice using input(), convert to int, store in choice

references.description: You wrote name = input('What is your name? '). Same pattern for the menu choice — just use int() to convert to a number.
You wrote f-strings with variables. Use that pattern to show each destination with its distance from your constants.

items: 3 distance constants (no Galveston)
Welcome banner, name input, and roll input
Destination menu displayed with distances
Variable choice storing the selection

content.body: An if-elif-else chain that sets 4 variables based on the choice: destination, distance, status, and message.

Until now your programs ran straight through — same code, same path, every time. if-elif-else is where that ends. The same program now produces different outcomes based on the input. Here's the frame that makes the structure click: if and elif are for handling specific conditions you've planned for. else is the garbage collector. It catches everything you didn't think of — the invalid inputs, the edge cases, the user typing "potato" when you asked for a number. Without else, those cases crash your program. With it, you stay in control.

content.quote: Took me embarrassingly long to get why every menu I wrote needed an else branch — my brain kept saying 'but I covered all the choices.' Then I shipped a build, a tester entered '4' instead of 1-3, and my code crashed live in front of the QA team. Now I write the else first. T-Bone's rule: defensive code isn't pessimism, it's professionalism.

references.description: Shows the if-elif-else pattern for handling multiple conditions. Each branch sets variables based on a score — you'll do the same based on a choice.

items: AFTER getting the choice, start your if-elif-else chain
For choice == 1 (Corpus): Set destination, distance (use constant), status to 'BEACH', and a fun message
For choice == 2 (Houston): Set the same 4 variables with Houston values, status 'PROGRESS'
For choice == 3 (Austin): Set the same 4 variables with Austin values, status 'DETOUR'
For else (invalid): Set destination to 'Nowhere', distance to 0, status to 'ERROR'

content.body: An if-else that checks if miles_remaining is >= 0 (made it!) or < 0 (fell short). Use abs() to show how many miles short.

Every interactive program needs a success/failure check. Did the user enter valid input? Did the API return data? Did the calculation produce a usable result? Here you're asking: did the roll cover the distance? The pattern is the same everywhere — check a condition, branch on the result, communicate the outcome to the user. Master this one and you have the shape of half the code you'll ever write.

content.quote: Remember absolute value from math class? The notation |-50| = 50 gives you the distance from zero, always positive. Python's abs() function does the same thing: abs(-50) gives you 50. When the player falls short, use it to show how many miles they missed by as a positive number. Note: abs() isn't in the Gaddis textbook, but your professor is introducing it here — so it's fair game for this assignment.

references.description: Shows if-else with different calculations in each branch. Same idea here — different output depending on whether you made it or not.

items: Calculate remaining miles: miles_remaining = miles_rolled - distance
Display "Heading to {destination}!" and the distance
Check if made it: write an if statement checking if miles_remaining >= 0
If yes (made it): print arrival message, display the status message variable, show miles to spare
If no (fell short): calculate miles_short using abs(), then print how many miles short

content.body: A clean summary showing all the trip details: driver, destination, distance, miles rolled, remaining, and status.

The Trip Summary is the player's receipt. It consolidates every decision and calculation into one clean record they can read at a glance. In a real game (or app), this is what gets saved, shared, or scored. Pay attention to alignment and spacing — format is the difference between a summary that looks polished and one that looks like raw debug output.

references.description: You wrote f-strings like print(f'Hello, {name}!'). Same pattern for each detail line — label followed by variable. And you already used '=' * 50 in Lab 1.1 for banners — same pattern here.

items: Print a blank line to separate from the arrival message
Print a banner line of 50 equal signs (use the '=' * 50 pattern from Lab 1.1)
Print "TRIP SUMMARY" as a heading
Print another banner line of 50 equal signs
Print 6 detail lines using f-strings: Driver, Destination, Distance, Miles rolled, Miles remaining, Status
Print a closing banner line of 50 equal signs
Print a final blank line

NOT permitted: loops, lists, functions you define, or any Chapter 4+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template you copy exactly — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: NameError: name 'SAT_TO_CORPUS' is not defined (or any constant)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Copy & Simplify) — a constant is missing or misspelled. The three required constants are SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, and SAT_TO_AUSTIN. Note that HOU_TO_GALVESTON from Lab 1.1 should be removed this unit.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: NameError: name 'HOU_TO_GALVESTON' is not defined or any Galveston-related variable
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Copy & Simplify) — you removed the constant but left a calculation or display line that still uses it. Search your file for "Galveston" or "HOU_" and remove every line.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: SyntaxError: expected ':' on one of the elif lines
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 (if-elif-else Chain) — every if, elif, and else line ends with a colon. Easy to miss, especially after else.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: NameError: name 'destination' (or distance, status, message) is not defined after the if-elif-else
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 (if-elif-else Chain) — one of your branches didn't set all four variables. Every branch (including else) needs all four assignments: destination, distance, status, message.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Output shows a negative miles short, like "You ran out of miles -50 miles from Houston"
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Did You Make It?) — you forgot to wrap the subtraction in abs(). The line should be miles_short = abs(miles_remaining).

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Player "makes it" when miles_remaining is exactly 0, but the test fails this case
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Did You Make It?) — verify your comparison is >= 0, not > 0. Hitting exactly the distance should count as arrival.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip Summary shows literal text like {destination} instead of the value
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 (Trip Summary) — you're missing the f prefix on an f-string. Every string with {} placeholders must start with f' or f".
